title: Homer Simpson In Kidney Trouble

jokes.text: Now with 30% more gunfights. And 40% more rootin'-tootin'. And the tumbleweeds tumble at 2:00, 4:00 and 6:00... plus a midnight tumbling on weekends.
jokes.setup: Ghost town tour begins with typical Wild West promises
jokes.punchline: Mechanical scheduling of 'authentic' Old West experiences like tumbleweeds
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons satire of tourist traps - the mechanical scheduling of 'authentic' experiences is absurd but believable

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Welcome to our authentic Old West ghost town experience. Every hour on the hour, a tumbleweed rolls through main street. At 2 PM, there's a mechanical tumbleweed. At 3 PM, another mechanical tumbleweed. At 4 PM, we have our premium mechanical tumbleweed with authentic squeaking sounds.

jokes.text: Three cheers for your father. Hip, hip- Mom, don't. Hip, hip- We heard you the first time. Hip, hip- Hey, I'm trying to drive here.
jokes.setup: Marge tries to get kids to cheer for Homer
jokes.punchline: Each family member shuts her down in succession
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure with character-specific responses - typical Simpson family dynamics

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Come on, kids! Let's all cheer for your father!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad's philosophy of life is to do whatever's convenient at the time.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!

jokes.text: Hey, Dad, that light says 'check engine.' Uh-oh. Tape must've fallen off.
jokes.punchline: Homer's solution is tape, not actual repair
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - ignoring problems with band-aid solutions

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A warning light? I've got just the thing.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you're not going to tape over it, are you?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Tape? No. I'm using duct tape.

jokes.text: There. Problem solved. [Engine Backfires] Oh, come on! Relax. She just needs a little lovin'.
jokes.punchline: Car immediately backfires, proving him wrong
jokes.explanation: Classic ironic timing - immediate contradiction of Homer's confidence

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Problem solved!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: BACKFIRE!

jokes.text: Ah, they remembered my birthday.
jokes.punchline: Homer interprets car's death rattle as birthday greeting
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - finding positive meaning in mechanical failure shows his delusional optimism

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh no, the car's making that noise again.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait... it's my birthday tomorrow.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The car's saying 'Happy Birthday, Homer!' How sweet!

jokes.text: Two hours? Why'd they build this ghost town so far away? Money. That's why everybody does everything.
jokes.setup: Homer complains about distance to ghost town
jokes.punchline: Lisa's cynical but accurate explanation
jokes.explanation: Lisa's world-weary wisdom contrasts with Homer's naivety - economical truth-telling

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why is the ghost town so far away?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Because it's a ghost town, Dad. It's not going to be conveniently located next to a Kwik-E-Mart.

jokes.text: Founded by prostitutes in 1849... and serviced by prostitute express riders... who could bring in a fresh prostitute from St.Joe in three days...
jokes.punchline: Every aspect of history reduced to prostitution
jokes.explanation: Repetitive structure builds absurdity of one-note historical narrative

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: And here we see the ancient Roman Forum, where Caesar himself once walked.

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Of course, what they don't teach you in school is that every aspect of history can be reduced to prostitution.

jokes.text: Three minutes! [Whistles] I never realized history was so filthy.
jokes.setup: Learning about quick spending in Old West
jokes.punchline: Homer's impressed whistle and Marge's prudish response
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate reactions - Homer impressed by speed, Marge by impropriety

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: *whistle of admiration*

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer!

jokes.text: Then we'll visit the cathouse, the brothel, the bordello... and finally the old mission. Oh, thank heaven! Lots of prostitutes in there.
jokes.setup: List of brothels ends with religious mission
jokes.punchline: Even the mission had prostitutes
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - raises hope for wholesome content then crushes it

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Even the mission had prostitutes

jokes.text: And these planks below us were often used as a sidewalk... by people who may or may not have been bandits.
jokes.setup: Tour guide points out wooden planks
jokes.punchline: Uncertain qualification about bandit usage
jokes.explanation: Mild satire of tour guide speculation - not particularly strong

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: And here we have the original wooden planks from the old bridge.

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: They were used by bandits... or so we think. We're not really sure.

jokes.text: Do we have to listen to you? Well, no, you don't. But if you have any interest in history- I'm done.
jokes.setup: Homer asks if tour is mandatory
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately opts out when given choice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - lazy and impatient when given any excuse

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Is this tour mandatory?

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: No, it's completely optional.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woohoo! I'm outta here!

jokes.text: Hey, robot, get your fat metal ass down here! First of all, I'm not a robot. And second, I got this metal ass in 'Nam... defending this country for lazy jerks like you.
jokes.setup: Homer insults what he thinks is a robot
jokes.punchline: Turns out to be disabled veteran
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that makes Homer look bad while creating sympathy for veteran

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, what's with the weird robot voice? You sound like a broken toaster!

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: That's my speech synthesizer. I'm a disabled veteran.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh... uh... thanks for your service?

jokes.text: Let's see. One, two, three- Six whiskeys. We only serve sarsaparilla, mac. No alcohol. [Groans] You can get drunk when we get home. Duh.
jokes.setup: Homer orders multiple whiskeys at Old West saloon
jokes.punchline: No alcohol served, Lisa's matter-of-fact 'duh'
jokes.explanation: Standard tourist trap reality vs. expectation, Lisa's casual enablement of Homer's drinking

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll take three whiskeys.

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: Sorry, we don't serve alcohol here.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Duh.

jokes.text: [Robot Voice] Hey, these cards are marked. Now look what you've done. I'm sorry. I don't know what came over me.
jokes.setup: Robot accuses of cheating at cards
jokes.punchline: Homer apologizes for robot malfunction he caused
jokes.explanation: Mild physical comedy setup with Homer taking responsibility for chaos

dialogue.speaker: Robot
dialogue.line: You cheated at cards!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm sorry! I spilled Duff beer on your circuits!

jokes.text: What the heck is this? A tea party? Somebody kill somebody! [Grunts]
jokes.punchline: Homer demands violence for authenticity
jokes.explanation: Homer's bloodlust for entertainment - wants authentic violence at family destination

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This saloon's too peaceful. We need some authentic Old West violence!

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: Sir, this is a family establishment.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Then get me to a bar where people actually fight!

jokes.text: What is it with you and robots?
jokes.punchline: Lisa notes Homer's pattern of robot aggression
jokes.explanation: Meta-joke acknowledging Homer's recurring robot conflicts across the series

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's the third robot you've attacked this week.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, they kept looking at me funny!

jokes.text: Oh, that's cute! Did you get it in the souvenir shop? No, that security guard is handing 'em out.
jokes.setup: Marge assumes Lisa bought a souvenir gun
jokes.punchline: Security guard giving out real weapons
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag setup - Marge's innocent assumption vs. dangerous reality

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Lisa, did you buy a souvenir gun?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No, Mom. The security guard is giving out real weapons.

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: Here you go, ma'am. One authentic firearm.

jokes.text: There's Old Curly. He played the town preacher until we laid him off. But he still hangs around. Help me, please. I'm sick. [Laughing]
jokes.punchline: Actual sick person being ignored as entertainment
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy - real suffering dismissed as performance, tourists' callousness

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: And here we have our former employee, Barney Gumble, suffering from what appears to be a severe case of alcohol poisoning.

dialogue.speaker: Tourists
dialogue.line: Ohhhhh!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Help me...

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: As you can see, he's still alive. Barely.

dialogue.speaker: Tourists
dialogue.line: Ahhhhh!

jokes.text: Homer! But it's funny, Marge. The guy's sick.
jokes.setup: Marge objects to laughing at sick man
jokes.punchline: Homer's twisted logic about why suffering is funny
jokes.explanation: Homer's callousness perfectly in character - finds suffering intrinsically amusing

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, how could you laugh at that poor man suffering?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, if we didn't laugh at people's misfortunes, what would be the point of having a TV?

jokes.text: It 'angries' up the blood. You like it, huh? Up yours!
jokes.punchline: Made-up word 'angeries' and immediate hostility
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons wordplay and sudden character mood shift - quotable invention

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Moe, I'd like a sarsaparilla.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: We don't have sarsaparilla.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But you have all those orangeries.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Orangeries?!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, those things oranges come from.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Get out!

jokes.text: Can I go to the bathroom before we leave? We gotta get home. I don't want to miss Inside the Actor's Studio. Tonight it's F. Murray Abraham. But I really need to- F. Murray Abraham!
jokes.setup: Grampa needs bathroom, Homer wants to leave
jokes.punchline: Homer's obsession with obscure actor overrides Grampa's needs
jokes.explanation: Great character comedy - Homer's random celebrity obsession vs. basic human needs

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Homer, I need to use the bathroom!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not now, Dad. Did you know that Bud Spencer made 47 spaghetti westerns? We need to discuss this immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: But Homer, I'm 81 years old!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Forty-seven! That's almost as many as there are kinds of donuts. This is important.

jokes.text: Can I go behind a tree? What are you? An animal?
jokes.punchline: Homer's misplaced dignity while denying basic needs
jokes.explanation: Homer's backwards priorities - more concerned with propriety than father's comfort

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: You know, back in my day we didn't need all these fancy laxatives. We just ate prunes and let nature take its course.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad, I'm not eating prunes. That's for old people.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: That's what I'm telling you - it works!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'd rather suffer.

jokes.text: Please go back! You can make it my birthday present. We're almost home, Dad. Only a couple more times over the horizon. But I might explode!
jokes.setup: Grampa pleads to stop for bathroom
jokes.punchline: Homer's vague distance estimate while Grampa reaches crisis
jokes.explanation: Building tension through Homer's obliviousness and Grampa's desperation

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Homer, I gotta use the bathroom!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We're only a few hours away.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: A few hours?! I can't hold it that long!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you're gonna have to.

jokes.text: You just sit back and relax. / I'm not gonna let anything happen to my old dad.
jokes.setup: Homer's promise to take care of his father
jokes.punchline: Promise delivered while actively causing harm through neglect
jokes.explanation: Perfect dramatic irony - loving words while committing harmful neglect

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I promise I'll take care of you, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: That's nice, boy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Now if you'll excuse me, I have to go.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Wait, where are you going? It's freezing and I'm still in my underwear!

jokes.text: This man's kidneys have exploded. There's nothing left. Oh, no! Yeah, that's what happens when you get older. It's one of those natural things. Beautiful in its way.
jokes.punchline: Homer's romanticizing of organ failure
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - Homer finding beauty in medical horror, typical shallow philosophy

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: I'm afraid you have a serious condition. Your liver is failing.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My liver? But I love my liver! It's been so good to me all these years.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Mr. Simpson, your liver is shutting down.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Shutting down? Like a computer? Can't you just turn it back on again?

jokes.text: Excuse me, Doctor. I think I know a little something about medicine. Homer, with all due respect, this X-ray reveals a textbook kidney blowout.
jokes.punchline: Doctor's polite but firm correction with technical terminology
jokes.explanation: Homer's unwarranted confidence vs. actual expertise, 'textbook kidney blowout' is absurd medical term

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm pretty sure I know more about medicine than you do, Doc.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Homer, I have a medical degree from Harvard University.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well I once cured my own hiccups by holding my breath and eating a whole jar of mayonnaise.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: That's not how the human respiratory or digestive systems work, Homer.

jokes.text: So you're saying I don't need a new muffler? I don't feel so good. Maybe I oughta eat something. Oh, I'm afraid your eating days are over. [Laughing]
jokes.setup: Homer connects kidney explosion to car problems
jokes.punchline: Doctor's casual cruelty about no more eating
jokes.explanation: Homer's mechanical thinking and Dr. Hibbert's callous amusement at patient suffering

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Doc, is there any connection between my kidney exploding and my car problems?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well Homer, I'm afraid you'll have to stop eating.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Stop eating?!

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: No, just the foods that are bad for you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh thank God.

jokes.text: How long do I have to live, Doc? [Laughs] I'm amazed you're alive now. Oh, I blame myself for this. We all blame you.
jokes.punchline: Family immediately blames Homer
jokes.explanation: Perfect structure - Homer's guilt met with unanimous family blame, economical and brutal

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: How long do you think I got, Doc?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, I'd say you've got about five good years left.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Five years? That's not so bad.

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: It's Homer's fault.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa Simpson
dialogue.line: Yeah, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: Homer!

jokes.text: Well, you could give him a kidney. A kidney? Okay, fine. I said fine. What is it about the word 'sure' you don't understand?
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate agreement escalates to impatience
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - impulsive decision-making and impatience with others' surprise

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Homer, would you consider donating one of your kidneys?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh sure, Doc! I'd be happy to!

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: That's wonderful! We'll need to run some tests first—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Tests? How long will that take?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: A few weeks, perhaps a month—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A month?! Just take it! Take it now! Rip it out!

jokes.text: Hmm, I'll take the left one. It's good and springy. Though the right one's not without its charms.
jokes.punchline: Shopping for organs like produce
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - treating organ harvest like grocery shopping, casual consumer language

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, your kidneys are in terrible shape. You'll need a transplant.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A transplant? But doc, I don't have that kind of money!

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: I'm afraid you'll have to shop around for organs, just like produce at the supermarket.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You mean I can squeeze 'em to see if they're ripe?

jokes.text: [Gasps] Apes-A-Poppin'! Whoo, the airline version.
jokes.setup: Homer excited about gorilla movie collection
jokes.punchline: Disappointment it's censored airline version
jokes.explanation: Good fake movie title and Homer's specific disappointment about censorship

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! A gorilla movie collection! This is gonna be great!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... this is the airline version. All the good stuff is cut out!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Ahh, I'm the luckiest man in the world... now that Lou Gehrig's dead.
jokes.setup: Homer feels like luckiest man alive
jokes.punchline: Only because famous 'luckiest man' is dead
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark comedy - subverts famous quote with morbid logic, perfect Homer selfishness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm the luckiest man alive!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what about that man they call 'the luckiest man alive'?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, he's dead.

jokes.text: No, no, no. Makin' polenta- that's a procedure. You're talkin' about deadly, life-threatening surgery here. / Really? Do you think it's dangerous?
jokes.punchline: Homer's naive surprise at surgery being dangerous
jokes.explanation: Homer's innocent obliviousness to obvious medical risks

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: It's not a 'little' cut, Homer. You nearly severed your arm off!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh come on, Moe. Surgery's dangerous?

jokes.text: It's not an operation, Moe. The doctor says it's just a procedure. No, no, no. Makin' polenta- that's a procedure. You're talkin' about deadly, life-threatening surgery here.
jokes.punchline: Moe's specific cooking comparison to show absurdity
jokes.explanation: Great contrast between Homer's denial and Moe's stark reality, polenta reference is specific and funny

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's not surgery, it's just a little procedure.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: A procedure? Homer, that's like me calling my chili a 'soup situation.' It doesn't make it less serious!

jokes.text: Plus they'll put you on one of those organ donor sucker lists. Everybody who wants an eyeball or a spine or a vestigial tail will be after you.
jokes.punchline: Escalates to absurd vestigial tail demand
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from plausible to absurd body parts, vestigial tail is unexpected

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: You're on the organ donor list? What, they gonna take your liver? Your kidneys? What?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, whatever they need.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not doing it. They can have my eyes, my heart, my spleen — but they ain't getting my vestigial tail!

jokes.text: Can I have your buttocks? I mean, if you die. They look pretty comfortable. And are those your original lips?
jokes.setup: Moe asks for Homer's body parts
jokes.punchline: Casual request for buttocks and lip authenticity question
jokes.explanation: Perfectly uncomfortable Moe behavior - inappropriate requests with casual delivery

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Give me your buttocks.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Is this lip real?

jokes.text: Oh, Homer, that was just a beer can with a whistle glued to it.
jokes.setup: Homer suggests his artificial kidney invention
jokes.punchline: Marge reveals it's just beer can and whistle
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer 'invention' - completely useless but he believes in it

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I've invented an artificial kidney!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's just a beer can and a whistle.

jokes.text: Oh, but I don't want them cutting up my soft, supple body. Why didn't somebody tell me what I was volunteering for?
jokes.setup: Homer realizing surgery involves actual cutting
jokes.punchline: Homer's vain self-description and feigned ignorance
jokes.explanation: Homer's narcissistic body image combined with willful ignorance about medical procedures

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, surgery? You mean they're gonna cut me open?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Yes, Mr. Simpson, that's what surgery is.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But I'm a very important man. Surely there's another way?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And what exactly is this 'surgery' thing anyway?

jokes.text: This is everybody's fault but mine.
jokes.setup: Homer facing consequences of his actions
jokes.punchline: Refuses all responsibility despite obvious guilt
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character line - pure deflection and self-preservation, very quotable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not responsible! It was an accident!

jokes.text: 'Jack and Jill went up the hill and- Jill came tumbling after.' The end. Good night.
jokes.punchline: Completely skips most of simple nursery rhyme
jokes.explanation: Homer's laziness even in simple parenting tasks - can't be bothered with full nursery rhyme

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Once upon a time, there was a cow who jumped over the moon. The end.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you skipped like the whole thing!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, you want the long version? Fine. Once upon a time, there was a cow... who jumped over the moon. Now go to sleep.

jokes.text: Say, how about a little NyQuil? [Gulps] All gone.
jokes.setup: Homer offers child medicine to help sleep
jokes.punchline: He's already drunk it all himself
jokes.explanation: Dark parenting humor - inappropriate medication offer plus Homer's selfishness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: He's already drunk it all himself

jokes.text: Those bedtime stories began my lifelong love affair with the printed word.
jokes.setup: Homer reflects on his terrible parenting
jokes.punchline: Claims it inspired love of reading despite being awful
jokes.explanation: Homer's delusional self-assessment - finds positive meaning in his laziness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, I may be a terrible father, but at least my kids love reading.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, they read all my angry letters from the bank.

jokes.text: But if I die during the operation, will you do one thing for me? Oh, anything, sweetheart. Blow up the hospital.
jokes.punchline: Requests terrorism against medical facility
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection from tender moment to violent revenge fantasy

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I have one last dying wish.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: What is it, Homer?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I want you to take me to that hospital and let me die there, so I can take out as many of those bastards as I can!

jokes.text: But if I die during the operation, will you do one thing for me? / Oh, anything, sweetheart. / Blow up the hospital.
jokes.punchline: Homer asking wife to commit terrorism
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection from romantic moment to absurd revenge fantasy

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, promise me you'll never remarry.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I promise.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: And promise me you'll never let anyone replace me as a mother to Bart, Lisa, and Maggie.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I promise.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: And promise me you'll do everything in your power to keep this family together.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I promise. Anything else?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yes. One more thing. Promise me you'll blow up the nuclear plant.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What?!

jokes.text: Hmm. Well, I said I'd do it, so I guess I'll have to. That's my girl.
jokes.punchline: Reluctantly agrees due to promise, Homer's pride
jokes.explanation: Marge's moral obligation to terrible promise shows her character's commitment

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I can't believe you're asking me to do this. It's dangerous and illegal.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, you promised! You said you'd do anything for me.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That was before you asked me to help you with your... scheme.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A promise is a promise, Marge. I'm counting on you.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Fine. FINE! I'll do it. But I'm not happy about it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's my wife! Woo-hoo!

jokes.text: Hmm. Well, I said I'd do it, so I guess I'll have to. / That's my girl.
jokes.punchline: Marge's reluctant agreement based on her word
jokes.explanation: Marge's moral integrity leading her to agree to terrorism is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, I need you to blow up the hospital.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I can't do that.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But you promised!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I promised to support you, not commit a felony.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A promise is a promise, Marge.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, all right. But this is the last time!

jokes.text: We gotta get rid of that window.
jokes.setup: Homer has escaped through hospital window
jokes.punchline: Doctor's practical response to security breach
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about hospital design flaw

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, I'm sure he had a very good reason for leaving. Probably realized he didn't have insurance.

dialogue.speaker: Nurse
dialogue.line: Doctor, he jumped out the window!

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Eh, he'll be back. They always come back. Usually in an ambulance.

jokes.text: I can't imagine Dad running away like this. / Leaving his father to die? Even I wouldn't do that. And I'm America's bad boy.
jokes.punchline: Bart's moral superiority despite his reputation
jokes.explanation: Bart's self-aware bad boy identity contrasted with having higher moral standards than his father

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: How could Dad just abandon us like that?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, man. That's pretty low. Even for me.

jokes.text: Even I wouldn't do that. And I'm America's bad boy.
jokes.punchline: Bart's self-aware assessment of his own badness
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware Bart moment - knows his reputation but has moral limits

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, at least I'm bad in a fun way.

jokes.text: Not like those mean old mountains. I hate them so much.
jokes.setup: Homer thinks the sea forgives all
jokes.punchline: Random hatred of mountains
jokes.explanation: Pure absurdist Homer logic - assigning personality traits to geographical features

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The sea? She's been good to me. She forgives all.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But mountains? Mountains are just angry rocks that won't leave me alone!

jokes.text: Well, if you look closely, you'll see this is a taffy shop shaped like a ship.
jokes.setup: Homer asks to join ship crew
jokes.punchline: It's just a candy shop designed to look like ship
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag setup and Homer's gullibility about obvious tourist trap

dialogue.speaker: Shop Owner
dialogue.line: It's just a candy shop designed to look like a ship.

jokes.text: Oh, I like taffy, but I don't deserve taffy.
jokes.punchline: Self-flagellation extends to candy denial
jokes.explanation: Homer's guilt manifested in candy abstinence - specific and pathetic

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A taffy shop? Oh boy!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, I'm on a diet. I can't go in there.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's self-flagellation through candy denial.

jokes.text: Krusty here to cheer you up... as part of the hospital's Last Laugh program. / Last laugh?
jokes.punchline: Program name implying death is imminent
jokes.explanation: Dark hospital humor with morbid program naming

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hey there, pal! Krusty the Clown's here to cheer ya up!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Oh, that's nice. What's that thing you're holding?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: This? It's a 'Get Well Soon' balloon!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: It says 'See You Later'...

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, the store was out of the cheerful ones.

jokes.text: Oh, boy! Ooh, looks like we're both short on time.
jokes.punchline: Casually acknowledges they're both dying soon
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy in hospital entertainment program - shared mortality as bonding

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Well, well, well... looks like we're both checking out of the same hotel soon, eh Grampa?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Yep, we're a couple of goners.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: At least we'll have company on the way down.

jokes.text: If you don't have a captain, I could be that. Arr! What other ships have you been on? I've been on that one. The taffy shop.
jokes.punchline: References previous taffy shop confusion as maritime experience
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to previous joke, Homer's delusional confidence in fake qualifications

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I've got plenty of maritime experience. Remember that time I worked in the taffy shop?

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: That was a taffy shop, not a ship!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Taffy, ship — they both have a lot of sticky situations.

jokes.text: Welcome aboard the ship of... lost souls. The name on the back says Honeybunch. Yarr, I've been meaning to paint over that.
jokes.setup: Captain dramatically introduces ship of lost souls
jokes.punchline: Ship actually named 'Honeybunch'
jokes.explanation: Great contrast between dramatic persona and cutesy boat name, undermines the mystique

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Ahoy! Behold this vessel of the damned, a ship of lost souls wandering the seven seas!

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: What's the name of this cursed ship, you ask? 'The Honeybunch.'

jokes.text: Who are we? No one. Where are we sailing? Nowhere. Do we even exist? Who knows?
jokes.punchline: Pretentious existential crisis
jokes.explanation: Parody of pretentious philosophical angst, French accent adds to stereotype

dialogue.speaker: French Sailor
dialogue.line: Bonjour, I am ze French Sailor. I exist, therefore I am... confused about why I exist in zis cartoon.

dialogue.speaker: French Sailor
dialogue.line: Ze meaning of life, it eludes me like ze American sense of culture.

jokes.text: Hey, let me off this thing. / Oh, don't listen to him. We exist all right. We wander the seven seas trying to forget. / Forget what?
jokes.setup: Sailor claiming they exist and trying to forget
jokes.punchline: Homer's practical question deflating dramatic setup
jokes.explanation: Homer's literalism puncturing romantic maritime angst

dialogue.speaker: English Sailor
dialogue.line: We exist only in the minds of others. I've been trying to forget that for years.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Have you tried beer?

jokes.text: If anyone has to go to the bathroom, go. I don't want you walking around during my story.
jokes.setup: Sailor about to tell dramatic story
jokes.punchline: Practical announcement about bathroom breaks
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of dramatic moment with practical concerns

dialogue.speaker: English Sailor
dialogue.line: Gather 'round, lads, and I'll tell ye a tale of the sea so dramatic and harrowing...

dialogue.speaker: English Sailor
dialogue.line: But first, anyone need to use the loo?

jokes.text: So one day I stole a loaf of bread... put it in the freezer until it was very hard, then robbed a bank with it.
jokes.setup: Classic 'stole bread for family' story setup
jokes.punchline: Used bread as weapon for worse crime
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of sympathy-seeking story - creative weapon choice and escalation to worse crime

dialogue.speaker: Sailor
dialogue.line: I stole bread to feed my family.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: That's a crime, but understandable. What happened next?

dialogue.speaker: Sailor
dialogue.line: I used that bread as a weapon to commit murder.

jokes.text: And when Mr. Dinkley saw what I had done... I was banned from the car wash forever.
jokes.punchline: Mundane car wash ban
jokes.explanation: Good anticlimax - builds expectation for serious consequence then delivers trivial one

dialogue.speaker: Sailor
dialogue.line: You'll never set foot in a car wash in this town again!

jokes.text: Come, stranger. Join our circle of infamy. Tell us your story of ennui.
jokes.punchline: Pretentious use of 'ennui'
jokes.explanation: Mild satire of pretentious vocabulary in dramatic situations

dialogue.speaker: Sailor
dialogue.line: Tell us your tragic story.

dialogue.speaker: Character
dialogue.line: Well, I suffered from a terrible case of ennui.

jokes.text: Good Lord! Oh, how could you! I think I'm going to be sick. I stole this accordion from a blind monkey. But you! [Spits] You disgust even me!
jokes.punchline: Homer's betrayal worse than stealing from blind monkey
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - establishes moral hierarchy where animal cruelty is less bad than family betrayal

dialogue.speaker: Sailor 1
dialogue.line: That's worse than the time we stole from the blind monkey!

dialogue.speaker: Sailor 2
dialogue.line: Yeah, but Homer's betrayal was even worse than that.

jokes.text: Good Lord! / Oh, how could you! / I think I'm going to be sick. / I stole this accordion from a blind monkey. But you! [Spits] You disgust even me!
jokes.setup: Homer's story of abandoning dying father
jokes.punchline: Criminals finding Homer's actions too immoral, despite their own crimes
jokes.explanation: Great escalation showing even criminals have standards Homer violates

dialogue.speaker: Sailor 1
dialogue.line: So you're telling us you abandoned your dying father?

dialogue.speaker: Sailor 2
dialogue.line: That's pretty low, even for us.

dialogue.speaker: Sailor 1
dialogue.line: Yeah, and we're criminals.

jokes.text: That's the last time I trust the strangest people on Earth.
jokes.setup: Homer thrown off ship by other misfits
jokes.punchline: Promises not to trust weird people while being weird himself
jokes.explanation: Homer's lack of self-awareness - doesn't recognize he's the weirdest of all

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I promise I'll never trust weird people again.

dialogue.speaker: Other Misfits
dialogue.line: You're weird!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, I'm not!

jokes.text: Even the sea won't forgive me.
jokes.punchline: Personifies sea as judgmental
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier belief that sea forgives all - now even that has abandoned him

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Even the sea is rejecting me. Look at it out there, judging me with its waves.

jokes.text: Larry Hagman took it. He's got five of them now. And three hearts. We didn't want to give them to him, but he overpowered us.
jokes.punchline: Larry Hagman hoarding organs through violence
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist celebrity reference - creates entire mythology around actor's organ collecting

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, I'm afraid there are no kidneys available right now. Apparently Larry Hagman's been buying them all up.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Larry Hagman? What's he need all those kidneys for?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, he's not exactly buying them. He's been going around to hospitals with a gun, demanding organ donations.

jokes.text: Shelbyville Hospital!
jokes.setup: Grampa says he's going to a better place
jokes.punchline: Just rival town's hospital
jokes.explanation: Subverts 'better place' death euphemism with mundane hospital transfer

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I'm going to a better place.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Just rival town's hospital.

jokes.text: Grave nothin'! I never felt more alive! / [Laughs] / [Bones Cracking] / Oh, boy, I shouldn't have done that.
jokes.punchline: Immediate physical injury from celebration
jokes.explanation: Standard ironic celebration followed by injury sequence

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I'm back, baby! Nothing can stop me now!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I'm gonna celebrate by doing a backflip!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: *attempts backflip and crashes into furniture*

jokes.text: While we were setting your broken bones and putting your blood back in... we helped ourselves to a kidney and gave it to your father.
jokes.punchline: Casual organ theft during emergency care
jokes.explanation: Dark medical comedy - treating organ harvesting as casual medical opportunism

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, we're going to need to perform emergency surgery. Don't worry, I've done this hundreds of times.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hundreds of times? That's great, Doc!

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, at least a dozen. Anyway, while we're in there, I might as well take out that kidney. I've got a buyer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, what?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: I'm joking! ...Mostly.

jokes.text: Don't you dance out that door, you-
jokes.punchline: Homer's impotent rage at dancing celebration
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy of old man celebrating while angry son watches helplessly

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad, you can't just dance around like that with a new kidney!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Why not? I'm alive, aren't I?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, but— but— ARGH!

jokes.text: Yes. You've shortened your life significantly... so someone else can have a slight extension of theirs.
jokes.punchline: Terrible cost-benefit analysis of organ donation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - medical professional casually describes terrible trade-off as positive

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, you've made a tremendous sacrifice. By donating your organs, you'll save up to eight lives.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Eight lives? That's great!

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Of course, you'll only get about fifty dollars per organ on the black market.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fifty dollars? But I need at least a hundred!

jokes.text: Yes, tickling.
jokes.setup: Homer says Grampa is tickling him during hug
jokes.punchline: Grampa's sinister acknowledgment
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable ending - suggests Grampa is getting revenge through inappropriate touching

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I'm not tickling you.

tmdb_overview: When Grandpa needs a new kidney, Homer agrees to donate one, but then chickens out.
insight: Kidney episode sustains 82.8 score on character comedy and dark subversive humor across 73 jokes.
